A **half marathon** is a [road running](/wiki/Road_running "Road running") event of 21.0975 kilometres (c. 13 mi 192.5 yd)—half the distance of a [marathon](/wiki/Marathon "Marathon").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is common for a half marathon event to be held concurrently with a marathon or a 5K race, using almost the same course with a late start, an early finish, or shortcuts.[[2]](#cite_note-2) If finisher medals are awarded, the medal or ribbon may differ from those for the full marathon. The half marathon is also known as a 21K, 21.1K, or 13.1 miles, although these values are rounded and not formally correct.[*[examples needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:AUDIENCE "Wikipedia:AUDIENCE")*]

A [half marathon world record](/wiki/Half_marathon_world_record_progression "Half marathon world record progression") is officially recognised by [World Athletics](/wiki/World_Athletics "World Athletics").[[3]](#cite_note-IAAF_men_record-3)[[4]](#cite_note-IAAF_women_record-4) The men's world record time is 57:30, set by Yomif Kejelcha in 2024 in [Valencia, Spain](/wiki/Valencia "Valencia"). [Jacob Kiplimo](/wiki/Jacob_Kiplimo "Jacob Kiplimo") ran 56:42 in Barcelona but this mark was not ratified by World Athletics due to his proximity to the lead car.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The women's world record, in a mixed-gender race, is 1:02:52, set by [Letesenbet Gidey](/wiki/Letesenbet_Gidey "Letesenbet Gidey") of Ethiopia in October 2021 in [Valencia, Spain](/wiki/Valencia "Valencia"). In a female-only race, the women's world record is 1:05:16, set by [Peres Jepchirchir](/wiki/Peres_Jepchirchir "Peres Jepchirchir") of Kenya in October 2020.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Participation in half marathons has grown steadily since 2003,[[7]](#cite_note-nytimes-7) partly because it is a challenging distance, but does not require the same level of training that a marathon does.[[7]](#cite_note-nytimes-7) In 2008, *Running USA* reported that the half marathon was the fastest-growing type of race.[[7]](#cite_note-nytimes-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Half_marathon&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The concept of the half-marathon emerged in the 1960s. The first half marathon was the [Route du Vin Half Marathon](/wiki/Route_du_Vin_Half_Marathon "Route du Vin Half Marathon") founded in 1961. Although the race was technically not the official current distance until it was standardized in 1995.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The event grew in popularity leading to the eventual establishment of the [World Athletics Road Running Championships](/wiki/World_Athletics_Road_Running_Championships "World Athletics Road Running Championships") in 1992, originally titled the IAAF World Half Marathon Championships.

The largest half marathon ever held was [Broloppet](/wiki/Broloppet "Broloppet") (the Bridge race) between [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen") in Denmark and [Malmö](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6 "Malmö") in Sweden with 79,719 finishers, held in connection with the [Øresund Bridge](/wiki/%C3%98resund_Bridge "Øresund Bridge") inauguration in 2000.[[58]](#cite_note-60)[[59]](#cite_note-61)
